lesson.title: Trend Following
lesson.body: Trend following (CTA-style) takes long positions when an asset is trending up and short when trending down. Classic systems: moving average crossovers (fast MA crosses above slow MA = buy signal), Donchian channel breakouts (buy when price breaks above N-day high). Trend following has positive skew and performs well during market crises — the opposite of carry/value strategies.

rooms.question: A moving average crossover signal goes long when:
answers: The fast MA crosses above the slow MA
The fast MA is below the slow MA
Price is below both MAs
The slow MA crosses above the fast MA
rooms.explanation: Golden cross: fast MA (e.g., 50-day) crosses above slow MA (e.g., 200-day) → bullish signal. Death cross is the reverse.

rooms.question: A Donchian channel breakout system buys when:
answers: Price exceeds the N-day high
Price falls below the N-day low
The middle channel is trending
Volume exceeds average
rooms.explanation: Donchian system: buy breakout above N-day high (e.g., 20-day high), sell breakout below N-day low. Made famous by the Turtle Traders.

rooms.question: Trend following strategies typically have which payoff profile?
answers: Many small losses, occasional large gains (positive skew)
Many small gains, occasional large losses (negative skew)
Symmetric gains and losses
Guaranteed positive returns
rooms.explanation: Trend following cuts losses quickly (small, frequent) and lets winners run (rare but large). This positive skew makes it uncorrelated with or negatively correlated with equity crises.

rooms.question: Order the moving average crossover system implementation:
items: Compute fast MA (e.g., 50-day) and slow MA (200-day)
Generate signal: +1 if fast > slow, −1 if fast < slow
Trade at close; rebalance when signal changes
rooms.explanation: Compute MAs, generate binary signal, volatility-scale for risk management, then trade.

rooms.question: The main cost of trend following is:
answers: Whipsaws: frequent false signals in range-bound markets lead to transaction costs
High data requirements
Negative expected return in trending markets
Regulatory restrictions
rooms.explanation: In sideways markets, MAs cross back and forth (whipsaws), generating many trades at a loss. Trend following needs sustained trends to be profitable.

boss.name: The Range-Bound Market
boss.taunt: There is no trend here. Only noise. Your moving average crossovers are lies.

questions.question: An exponentially weighted moving average (EMA) with decay factor λ weights recent prices:
answers: More than older prices (exponentially decaying weights)
Equally with older prices
Less than older prices
Only based on the last price
questions.explanation: EMA = λP_t + (1−λ)EMA_{t-1}. Weights decay exponentially backward in time. EMA is more responsive to recent changes than SMA.

questions.question: Trend following strategies are known for their positive performance during equity market drawdowns because:
answers: They are typically short equities during sustained downtrends
They hold no equity exposure
They use options for protection
Fixed income exposure provides a hedge
questions.explanation: CTAs go short when equity trends turn negative. During 2008, 2001-2002 crashes, trend-following funds posted positive returns by riding the downtrend.

questions.question: The optimal lookback period for a trend-following MA crossover:
answers: Depends on the asset and varies over time — there is no universal optimum
Is always 50/200 days
Is shorter for equity than commodities
Should be 10/50 days to maximize Sharpe ratio
questions.explanation: Different assets trend at different speeds. Optimizing lookbacks in-sample leads to overfitting. Robust trend systems use multiple lookbacks and average signals.
